county_metadata.grandparent_county: Original province (one of five original counties)
county_metadata.record_losses: No significant record losses. County records generally well preserved since formation.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Strafford County in 1840. Named for Jeremy Belknap, author of 'History of New Hampshire.' Located in the Lakes Region around Lake Winnipesaukee. Originally part of Strafford County from 1771.
county_metadata.research_tips: Belknap County deeds are held at the Register of Deeds in Laconia — the grantor/grantee indexes go back to 1840, but for pre-1840 land transfers check Strafford County records in Dover. The Lakes Region attracted summer tourism by the 1850s, generating hotel registers and resort community records now held at the Belknap Mill Society and Laconia Public Library. Town clerks in Gilford, Meredith, and Laconia hold vital records (births/marriages/deaths) from the early 1700s. The Congregational and Baptist churches in Gilmanton and Sanbornton have exceptionally complete membership and baptismal records from the late 1700s. NHHS in Concord holds manuscript collections relevant to Belknap County families. Lake Winnipesaukee island deeds are recorded in Belknap County and can be tricky — check Tuftonboro (Carroll County) for eastern shore properties.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Grantor/grantee index and scanned images via NH Deeds portal

source_profiles.repository: Belknap County Register of Deeds, Laconia
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records from county formation. Pre-1840 records in Strafford County.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — no losses.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate files, wills, administrations, guardianships from 1840. Pre-1840 probate in Strafford County.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well preserved. Original files at county courthouse.

source_profiles.source_name: New Hampshire Vital Records to 1901 — Belknap County Towns
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Town-level vital records for Belknap County towns including Laconia, Gilford, Meredith, and Gilmanton. Part of the published NH Vital Records to 1901 series.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent — New England town clerk tradition preserved records from early settlement.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal population schedules 1840-1950 (1890 destroyed). Earlier censuses under Strafford County.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete for available census years. 1890 census destroyed.

source_profiles.source_name: Congregational and Baptist Church Records — Gilmanton and Sanbornton
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Membership rolls, baptisms, marriages, and deaths from Congregational churches in Gilmanton and Baptist churches in Sanbornton.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well preserved — original registers at NHHS or individual churches.

source_profiles.source_name: Laconia Daily Sun and Lakes Region newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Digitized historic newspapers from the Lakes Region including legal notices, obituaries, and town meeting reports.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; microfilm copies at NH State Library for additional coverage.

source_profiles.repository: NH Division of Archives and Records Management (DARM), Concord
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Property tax lists and assessments for Belknap County towns. Useful for tracking land ownership between deed transactions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Spotty — survival varies by town. Best for Laconia and Gilmanton.

county_metadata.grandparent_county: Original province (one of five original counties)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Strafford County in 1840. Named for Charles Carroll of Carrollton, signer of the Declaration of Independence. Includes the White Mountains resort area (Conway, North Conway, Bartlett). Eastern shore of Lake Winnipesaukee partially in Carroll County (Wolfeboro, Tuftonboro).
county_metadata.research_tips: Carroll County is the heart of White Mountains tourism — hotel employment records, resort guest registers, and seasonal worker documentation can be valuable for tracing 19th-century families. The Carroll County Register of Deeds in Ossipee has records from 1840; pre-1840 land records are in Strafford County (Dover). Wolfeboro claims to be 'America's Oldest Summer Resort' (Gov. Wentworth's 1768 estate), and early Wolfeboro town records are rich sources. Quaker meetings existed in several Carroll County towns and their records are at NEYM archives. The Conway and Tamworth historical societies hold local family files. Check Dartmouth College's Rauner Library for White Mountains tourism-era manuscript collections. FamilySearch has microfilmed NH town vital records covering Carroll County towns from the 1700s.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Grantor/grantee index and scanned images via NH Deeds portal

source_profiles.repository: Carroll County Register of Deeds, Ossipee
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records from county formation. Pre-1840 records in Strafford County.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — no losses.

source_profiles.source_name: New Hampshire Marriage Records — Carroll County Towns
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Town-level marriage returns for Carroll County communities. Part of the published NH Vital Records to 1901.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good coverage from town records; some gaps in earliest period.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, inventories, guardianships from 1840.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — well maintained at courthouse.

source_profiles.source_name: NH Adjutant General Reports — Carroll County Soldiers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil War enlistment records for Carroll County men from NH Adjutant General published reports.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Published records complete; individual soldier files may be at NARA.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Transcribed inscriptions from cemeteries throughout Carroll County, including small family burying grounds in rural towns.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Ongoing transcription project; early stones weathered in some locations.

source_profiles.source_name: New Hampshire Death Records — Carroll County
source_profiles.repository: NH DARM / NH Division of Vital Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Town-level death records from Carroll County towns, compiled in the NH Vital Records to 1901 series.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — New England town clerk records well preserved.

county_metadata.parent_county: Original province (one of five original counties)
county_metadata.record_losses: No significant record losses. Records well preserved since colonial era.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of the five original New Hampshire counties created in 1769 when the province was divided. Named for Cheshire in England. Southwest corner of NH, bordering Vermont and Massachusetts. Keene is the regional hub of the Monadnock region.
county_metadata.research_tips: Cheshire County is one of NH's five original counties (1769), so its deeds and probate go back to the colonial period — the Register of Deeds in Keene has records from the 1760s. The Monadnock region was settled by families from Connecticut and Massachusetts in the 1730s-1760s; trace migrations from those colonies. The Historical Society of Cheshire County in Keene holds extensive manuscript collections, including the Keene city records, business ledgers, and family genealogies. Keene's glass and pottery industries generated industrial employment records. The Universalist and Congregational churches in Keene, Walpole, and Swanzey have substantial baptismal and membership records. Check the NH Vital Records to 1901 for town returns; the Swanzey town records are unusually complete from 1737. Walpole's early settlers included prominent families connected to the Connecticut River Valley's economic networks.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Grantor/grantee index and scanned images via NH Deeds portal

source_profiles.repository: Cheshire County Register of Deeds, Keene
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Colonial-era through modern land records. One of the oldest continuous deed series in NH.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — colonial volumes well preserved.

source_profiles.repository: Cheshire County Probate Court, Keene / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, inventories, and administrations from colonial period through early 20th century. FamilySearch has microfilm of early volumes.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well preserved — continuous series from 1769.

source_profiles.source_name: New Hampshire Vital Records to 1901 — Cheshire County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Town clerk vital records from Keene, Walpole, Swanzey, and other Cheshire County towns. Swanzey records date from 1737.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent — some of the oldest town records in NH.

source_profiles.source_name: Universalist and Congregational Church Records — Monadnock Region
source_profiles.repository: Historical Society of Cheshire County / NHHS
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Membership rolls, baptisms, marriages from Congregational and Universalist churches in Keene, Walpole, and Swanzey.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival — deposited at historical society.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Court of Common Pleas and Superior Court records including civil suits, divorces, and criminal cases.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Colonial-era court records transferred to DARM; good condition.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Town tax assessments and ratepayer lists useful for identifying residents between census years.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Varies by town; Keene and Walpole have better survival.

source_profiles.repository: Chronicling America / Historical Society of Cheshire County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: One of NH's longest-running newspapers. Vital notices, legal ads, estate settlements, and local news.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partially digitized; complete microfilm run at Historical Society of Cheshire County.

county_metadata.grandparent_county: Original province (one of five original counties)
county_metadata.record_losses: No significant courthouse losses. Some early town records sparse due to late settlement of remote areas.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Grafton County in 1803. Named from an Abenaki word meaning 'place of pines.' Largest county in NH by area, covering the entire North Country including the White Mountains, Dixville Notch, and the Connecticut River headwaters. Sparsely populated with extensive unincorporated areas (townships, grants, purchases).
county_metadata.research_tips: Coos County was the last region of NH settled and presents unique challenges — many areas remained unincorporated well into the 19th century and have no town clerk records. The Register of Deeds in Lancaster holds records from 1803; earlier land grants are in Grafton County or in the original Masonian Proprietors records at DARM. The lumber and paper industries dominated Coos County's economy, and Berlin's mill employment records (Brown Company, later James River Corporation) at the Berlin and Coos County Historical Society are essential for tracing French Canadian immigrant families who migrated from Quebec. Catholic parish registers in Berlin (St. Anne, Guardian Angel) are critical for Franco-American genealogy. The Northern NH town clerk records are often sparse; supplement with church, census, and military records. Dartmouth College's Rauner Library holds White Mountains-related manuscripts.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Grantor/grantee index and scanned images via NH Deeds portal

source_profiles.repository: Coos County Register of Deeds, Lancaster
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records from county formation. Pre-1803 records in Grafton County.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — no losses.

source_profiles.source_name: New Hampshire Vital Records — Coos County Towns
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Town vital records for Lancaster, Gorham, Berlin, and other Coos County towns. Late settlement means fewer early records than southern NH.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good for established towns; sparse for unincorporated areas.

source_profiles.source_name: Catholic Parish Registers — Berlin (St. Anne, Guardian Angel)
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Baptisms, marriages, and burials from French Canadian Catholic parishes in Berlin. Essential for Franco-American genealogy in the North Country.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Sacramental registers well preserved; some microfilmed by FamilySearch.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Declarations of intention and petitions from Coos County courts. French Canadian, Scandinavian, and Eastern European immigrants.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — housed at DARM and NARA.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal population schedules from 1810 (first census after county formation). Captures lumber camp and mill worker populations.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete for available census years. 1890 census destroyed.

source_profiles.source_name: Masonian Proprietors and NH Land Grants — Coos County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Original township grants and lot divisions for Coos County settlements, including Masonian proprietors grants and state grants to settlers.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Provincial and state records at DARM; some published in NH State Papers.

county_metadata.parent_county: Original province (one of five original counties)
county_metadata.record_losses: No significant record losses. Early colonial records well preserved.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of the five original New Hampshire counties created in 1769. Named for Augustus Henry Fitzroy, 3rd Duke of Grafton, British PM. Second largest county in NH. Home of Dartmouth College in Hanover. Connecticut River border with Vermont. County seat has been in Haverhill/North Haverhill area since formation; courthouse in Woodsville.
county_metadata.research_tips: Grafton County has records from 1769, making it one of the richest in NH for colonial-era research. The Register of Deeds in Woodsville holds the earliest volumes. Dartmouth College's Rauner Special Collections Library in Hanover is a major manuscript repository with extensive northern NH family papers, town histories, and Connecticut River Valley documents — always check their holdings. The Upper Connecticut River Valley was settled from Connecticut via the 'River Towns' migration; trace families south through Wethersfield and Hartford CT. Lebanon and Hanover vital records extend to the 1760s. Grafton County was the parent of Coos County (1803), so pre-1803 North Country records are here. The Haverhill Corner Historic District has buildings dating to the 1780s and the local historical society holds court and town records. Shaker community records from Enfield (1793-1923) are at NHHS and the Enfield Shaker Museum.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Grantor/grantee index and scanned images via NH Deeds portal

source_profiles.repository: Grafton County Register of Deeds, Woodsville
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Colonial-era through modern land records. Parent county for all of northern NH until 1803.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — colonial volumes well preserved.

source_profiles.source_name: New Hampshire Vital Records to 1901 — Grafton County Towns
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Death records from Grafton County towns including Lebanon, Hanover, Plymouth, and Haverhill.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent — New England town clerk tradition.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate files from colonial period. FamilySearch has microfilm of early volumes.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well preserved continuous series.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Membership rolls, journals, and family records from the Enfield Shaker community. Documents incoming converts and their families of origin.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well preserved — divided between NHHS and Enfield Shaker Museum.

source_profiles.source_name: NH Adjutant General Reports — Grafton County Soldiers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Revolutionary War muster rolls and Civil War enlistments for Grafton County. Published NH Adjutant General reports are digitized.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Published volumes complete; original muster rolls at DARM.

source_profiles.source_name: Dartmouth College Rauner Library — Northern NH Manuscripts
source_profiles.repository: Rauner Special Collections, Dartmouth College, Hanover
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Family papers, estate documents, business records, and correspondence from Grafton County and Upper Connecticut River Valley families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Archival-quality preservation. Finding aids online.

source_profiles.source_name: NH Provincial Land Grants — Grafton County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Original Masonian Proprietors grants, Wentworth charters, and early town proprietors records for Grafton County townships.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Published in NH State Papers; originals at DARM.

county_metadata.parent_county: Original province (one of five original counties)
county_metadata.record_losses: No significant record losses. Two districts maintain separate deed registries.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of the five original NH counties created in 1769. Named for Wills Hill, Earl of Hillsborough, British Secretary of State. Most populous NH county containing Manchester (state's largest city) and Nashua. UNIQUE: Hillsborough County has two Register of Deeds offices — Northern District (Manchester) and Southern District (Nashua). Must check both depending on town location.
county_metadata.research_tips: Hillsborough County is NH's population center and has the most complex genealogical landscape. CRITICAL: The county has TWO separate Register of Deeds offices — the Northern District in Manchester and the Southern District in Nashua. Determine which district your town falls in before searching deeds. Manchester's Amoskeag Manufacturing Company records (1831-1936) at the Manchester Historic Association are essential for tracing mill worker families — French Canadian, Irish, Greek, and Polish immigrants all came to work the mills. The Manchester City Library has extensive local history and genealogy collections. Nashua similarly attracted immigrant labor (French Canadian and Irish especially). The Franco-American Centre in Manchester preserves French-language records. Catholic parish registers (St. Augustine, Ste. Marie, St. George Greek Orthodox) are critical sources for immigrant families. The Molly Stark Chapter of the DAR compiled extensive early family records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Two districts — Northern (Manchester) and Southern (Nashua) — both accessible via NH Deeds portal

source_profiles.source_name: Hillsborough County Register of Deeds — Northern and Southern Districts
source_profiles.repository: Hillsborough County Register of Deeds, Manchester & Nashua
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Two separate registries — Northern District (Manchester) and Southern District (Nashua). Colonial-era through modern records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — no losses in either district.

source_profiles.source_name: Franco-American Centre & French Canadian Parish Records — Manchester/Nashua
source_profiles.repository: Franco-American Centre, Manchester / Diocese of Manchester
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Records of French Canadian immigrant communities in Manchester and Nashua. Parish registers, mutual aid society records, and French-language newspapers.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good preservation — parish registers in diocesan archives, cultural records at Franco-American Centre.

source_profiles.source_name: New Hampshire Vital Records to 1901 — Hillsborough County Towns
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Town vital records from Hillsborough County towns. Amherst, Dunstable (now Nashua), and Bedford have records from the 1730s.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent coverage for established towns.

source_profiles.source_name: Federal Census — Hillsborough County (Manchester and Nashua enumeration)
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census from 1790 (1770s colonial census fragments). Manchester enumerations are large and complex due to immigrant boarding houses.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete for standard years. 1890 destroyed.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Declarations of intention and petitions from Hillsborough County courts. Heavy French Canadian, Irish, Greek, and Polish naturalizations.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — major series at DARM and NARA New England Region.

source_profiles.source_name: Manchester Union Leader and Nashua Telegraph
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Major daily newspapers covering Manchester and Nashua. Obituaries, marriage notices, court proceedings, and business news.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partially digitized; complete microfilm runs at Manchester City Library.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Superior Court civil and criminal records, divorce proceedings, and naturalization petitions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Older records transferred to DARM. Good condition.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Cemetery transcriptions including Pine Grove Cemetery (Manchester), Woodlawn Cemetery (Nashua), and older burying grounds.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — extensive transcription efforts by local societies.

county_metadata.grandparent_county: Original province (one of five original counties)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed in 1823 from parts of Rockingham and Hillsborough counties. Named for the Merrimack River. Contains Concord, the state capital, which is home to DARM, NHHS, and the state government complex. Central NH location.
county_metadata.research_tips: Merrimack County contains Concord, the state capital, which means the NH Division of Archives and Records Management (DARM) and the New Hampshire Historical Society (NHHS) are both physically in this county — researchers should visit both. DARM holds state vital records, provincial papers, and transferred county court records. NHHS has the state's premier genealogical library with manuscript collections, published genealogies, and the Tuck Library. The Concord Public Library has an excellent local history room. The Register of Deeds in Concord has records from 1823; for earlier land records check both Rockingham and Hillsborough counties depending on the town's location before 1823. Franklin (formerly Salisbury) was Daniel Webster's birthplace and has well-documented early families. Canterbury Shaker Village records (1792-1992) are at NHHS and the Shaker Village archives. Penacook/Boscawen early settlement records date from the 1720s.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Grantor/grantee index and scanned images via NH Deeds portal

source_profiles.repository: Merrimack County Register of Deeds, Concord
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records from county formation. Pre-1823 records split between Rockingham and Hillsborough counties.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — no losses.

source_profiles.source_name: New Hampshire Vital Records to 1901 — Merrimack County Towns
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Town marriage records from Concord, Boscawen, Canterbury, Salisbury (now Franklin), and other Merrimack County towns.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent — published in NH Vital Records to 1901 series.

source_profiles.repository: Merrimack County Probate Court, Concord / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, inventories, guardianships. Pre-1823 probate split between Rockingham and Hillsborough counties.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well preserved. FamilySearch has microfilm of older volumes.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Records of one of NH's two Shaker communities. Membership journals, family records, deaconess and eldress correspondence.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Exceptionally well preserved — Shakers were meticulous record keepers.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Revolutionary War through WWII military records for all NH counties, headquartered in Concord. Includes pension files, muster rolls, and draft registrations.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — state-level compilation.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Major Concord newspapers covering state capital news, legislative proceedings, obituaries, and legal notices.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partially digitized; microfilm at Concord Public Library.

county_metadata.parent_county: Original province (one of five original counties)
county_metadata.record_losses: No significant record losses. Some of NH's oldest surviving records are here.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of the five original NH counties created in 1769. Named for Charles Watson-Wentworth, 2nd Marquess of Rockingham, British PM. SE New Hampshire including the seacoast (Portsmouth), Exeter, and the Massachusetts border towns. Second most populous county. Contains Portsmouth, NH's original colonial capital and major seaport.
county_metadata.research_tips: Rockingham County is the historical heart of New Hampshire — Portsmouth was the colonial capital and its records are among the oldest in the state. The Register of Deeds in Brentwood (formerly Kingston) has records from 1769, but Portsmouth town records and provincial records at DARM are much older (1630s). The Scotch-Irish settlement at Londonderry (1719) is extremely well documented; the Londonderry Historical Society and published works by Edward Parker track these Presbyterian families. Portsmouth Athenaeum holds maritime, mercantile, and family papers from the colonial seaport. Strawbery Banke Museum in Portsmouth preserves neighborhood history. The Exeter town records are among NH's most complete, going back to the 1630s (Exeter was one of the four original towns). Check FamilySearch for microfilmed NH town vital records. The NH Society of Genealogists in Stratham has a research library specifically focused on southeastern NH families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Grantor/grantee index and scanned images via NH Deeds portal

source_profiles.repository: Rockingham County Register of Deeds, Brentwood
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Colonial-era through modern land records. NH's most populous coastal county with extensive real property transactions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — no losses. Some of NH's oldest deed volumes.

source_profiles.source_name: New Hampshire Vital Records to 1901 — Rockingham County Towns
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Some of NH's earliest vital records. Portsmouth, Exeter, Hampton, and Dover (now Strafford) records from the 1600s.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent — Exeter and Hampton town records among the oldest in New England.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, inventories from county formation. Earlier probate (pre-1769) handled by the Province of NH and recorded in provincial records at DARM.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well preserved continuous series. FamilySearch has microfilm.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Records of the Scotch-Irish settlement at Nutfield/Londonderry (1719). Presbyterian church membership, session minutes, and family records. One of the best-documented ethnic settlements in colonial New England.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — extensively published and transcribed by Edward Parker and others.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Muster rolls, pension applications, and bounty land records for Rockingham County Revolutionary War soldiers. NH Adjutant General reports digitized.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — published rolls and NARA pension files.

source_profiles.source_name: Portsmouth Athenaeum — Maritime and Family Papers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Manuscript collections of Portsmouth merchant families, ship logs, business ledgers, and personal correspondence. Critical for colonial-era Portsmouth families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent archival preservation. Finding aids available.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Transcriptions from some of NH's oldest burial grounds including Point of Graves (Portsmouth, 1671) and Hampton colonial cemetery.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Early stones weathered but extensively transcribed.

county_metadata.parent_county: Original province (one of five original counties)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of the five original NH counties created in 1769. Named for William Wentworth, 2nd Earl of Strafford. Eastern NH including Dover (one of NH's four original towns, settled 1623). Parent county for Belknap (1840) and Carroll (1840). Was originally much larger, covering most of eastern NH north of Rockingham.
county_metadata.research_tips: Strafford County contains Dover, one of NH's four original towns (settled 1623), making it one of the state's richest areas for early records. The Register of Deeds in Dover has records from 1769, and Dover's own town records predate the county by over a century. Dover was a major mill town — the Cocheco Manufacturing Company (1812-1909) records at the Woodman Institute Museum and Dover Public Library document early industrial workers including Irish and French Canadian immigrants. Strafford County was the parent of both Belknap and Carroll counties (1840), so pre-1840 records for those areas are in Dover. University of New Hampshire in Durham (Strafford County) holds regional manuscript collections in Dimond Library Special Collections. The early Quaker presence in Dover is well documented — Dover Monthly Meeting records are at NEYM archives. Rochester, Farmington, and Somersworth mill records complement Dover's industrial documentation.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Grantor/grantee index and scanned images via NH Deeds portal

source_profiles.repository: Strafford County Register of Deeds, Dover
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Colonial-era through modern land records. Parent county for Belknap and Carroll — pre-1840 eastern NH deeds here.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — no losses. One of NH's oldest deed registries.

source_profiles.source_name: New Hampshire Vital Records to 1901 — Strafford County Towns
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Town-level death records from Dover, Rochester, Somersworth, and other Strafford County towns. Dover records among the oldest in NH.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent — continuous town clerk records from 1600s.

source_profiles.repository: Strafford County Probate Court, Dover / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, inventories. Pre-1769 probate in provincial records at DARM. Covers much of eastern NH until 1840.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well preserved. FamilySearch has microfilm of early volumes.

source_profiles.repository: New England Yearly Meeting Archives / NHHS
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Records of the Dover Quaker meeting including births, marriages, deaths, removals, and meeting minutes. One of NH's earliest religious communities.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well preserved — Quakers kept meticulous records. Deposited at NEYM archives.

source_profiles.repository: Woodman Institute Museum / Dover Public Library
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Employment records, payroll books, and boarding house registers from Dover's major textile mill. Documents immigrant worker families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial — some records at Woodman Institute, others at Dover Public Library.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal population schedules 1790-1950. Note: pre-1840 Strafford County was much larger, including present-day Belknap and Carroll counties.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete for standard years. 1790 NH census survived (many states did not). 1890 destroyed.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Colonial and early republic tax lists for Dover, Rochester, and other Strafford County towns.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Variable by town; Dover has better survival than rural towns.

county_metadata.grandparent_county: Original province (one of five original counties)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Cheshire County in 1827. Named for General John Sullivan, Revolutionary War officer and first president (governor) of New Hampshire. Western NH along the Connecticut River, bordering Vermont. Claremont is the largest town.
county_metadata.research_tips: Sullivan County was carved from Cheshire County in 1827, so pre-1827 deeds and probate are in Keene (Cheshire County). The Register of Deeds in Newport has records from 1827. The Connecticut River Valley towns (Claremont, Cornish, Plainfield, Charlestown) were part of the 'River Towns' settlement pattern connecting to Connecticut and Massachusetts. Claremont had early industrial development and attracted Irish and French Canadian workers — check Catholic parish records (St. Mary, Claremont). Cornish was home to the Cornish Art Colony (1885-1935) and Augustus Saint-Gaudens' estate (now a National Historic Site); colony records document artists' families. The Sullivan County Historical Society in Newport has local family files. Charlestown was a frontier garrison town during the French and Indian Wars — Fort No. 4 records document military families and captivity narratives. The town of Acworth has unusually complete Congregational church records from 1772.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Grantor/grantee index and scanned images via NH Deeds portal

source_profiles.repository: Sullivan County Register of Deeds, Newport
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records from county formation. Pre-1827 records in Cheshire County (Keene).
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — no losses.

source_profiles.source_name: New Hampshire Vital Records to 1901 — Sullivan County Towns
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Town marriage records from Claremont, Newport, Charlestown, and other Sullivan County towns.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good coverage from town clerk records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, guardianships from county formation. Pre-1827 probate in Cheshire County.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well maintained at courthouse.

source_profiles.source_name: Fort No. 4 Records and French and Indian War Documents — Charlestown
source_profiles.repository: Fort at No. 4 Living History Museum / NH DARM
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Military garrison records, scalp bounty payments, captivity accounts, and provincial military correspondence related to the Charlestown frontier during King George's War and French and Indian War.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Provincial records at DARM; some published in NH State Papers.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Unusually complete Congregational church records including membership, baptisms, marriages, and disciplinary proceedings for Acworth and nearby towns.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well preserved — deposited at NHHS.

source_profiles.source_name: Claremont Eagle and Sullivan County newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Local newspapers with obituaries, legal notices, and community news for Sullivan County towns.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; microfilm at NH State Library.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal population schedules from 1830 (first census after county formation). Earlier census records under Cheshire County.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete for standard years. 1890 destroyed.
